title: Mapping Workflow
text: This page documents the current mapping workflow in this repository.

title: Mapping Workflow
text:
Overview

Genome download and indexing are handled by ./scripts/bcfngs.
Mapping is still run directly through the STAR Snakefiles already present in the repository:

Star.MappingQuant.PairedEndFastq.Snakefile
Star.MappingQuant.SingleEndFastq.Snakefile

Current mapping support is STAR-based. The genome preparation workflow creates additional bwa and bowtie2 indices for future use, but those mappers are not yet wired into a mapping wrapper.

Output layout:
<workdir>/<project>/<organism>/star_mapping/<sample>.bam
<workdir>/<project>/<organism>/star_mapping/<sample>.bam.bai
<workdir>/<project>/<organism>/bigwig/<sample>.bw
The STAR workflow also keeps:

<sample>.counts.tab
<sample>.SJ.out.tab

title: Mapping Workflow
text:
Required configuration
Update configs/run.yaml:
project: "P000"
path: "/fs/pool/pool-bcfngs/fastq_files/P000/P000_Testrun/conc.fastq/"
outdir: "/fs/pool/pool-bcfngs/mapping"
organism: "Caenorhabditis_elegans"
threads: 16
ram_bytes: 168632718037
organism must be one organism key from configs/genomes.yaml, using the scientific-name format such as Homo_sapiens or Mus_musculus.

title: Mapping Workflow
text:
FASTQ pattern auto-detection
The legacy STAR Snakefiles expect concatenated FASTQ files in the working directory. The important patterns are:

*.conc.R1.fastq.gz
*.conc.R2.fastq.gz for paired-end runs

The single-end Snakefile ignores R2, while the paired-end Snakefile requires both mates.

title: Mapping Workflow
text:
Basic usage
Dry-run first:
cd /fs/pool/pool-bcfngs/fastq_files/P000/P000_Testrun/conc.fastq/

# paired-end
snakemake -nps /path/to/repo/Star.MappingQuant.PairedEndFastq.Snakefile -j 20

# single-end
snakemake -nps /path/to/repo/Star.MappingQuant.SingleEndFastq.Snakefile -j 20
Remove -n after the dry-run looks correct.

title: Mapping Workflow
text:
Notes

Run ./scripts/bcfngs env check first to confirm the required tools are available in ~/miniconda3/envs/update_genomes.
The mapping Snakefiles use the stable STAR index symlink under /fs/pool/pool-bcfngs/genomes/<Organism>/starIndex.
wigToBigwig and bam2wig.py must be available in the same environment.

title: Mapping Workflow
text:
Flag meanings

-n: dry-run
-p: print shell commands
-s: snakefile path
-j: number of cores/jobs

title: Environment (micromamba)
text: This workflow uses micromamba to create a reproducible environment with all required tools.

title: Environment (micromamba)
text:
Default environment location
The commands in this repository assume this default path:
~/miniconda3/envs/update_genomes
On the HPC cluster this expands to:
/fs/gpfs41/lv07/fileset07/home/b_cox/yeroslaviz/miniconda3/envs/update_genomes
The wrapper ./scripts/bcfngs uses the same path by default. If you keep the environment somewhere else, add --env-prefix /path/to/env.

title: Environment (micromamba)
text:
Installing micromamba
micromamba can be installed from the github repository.
"${SHELL}" <(curl -L https://micro.mamba.pm/install.sh)

title: Environment (micromamba)
text:
Create the workflow environment
From the repo root:
micromamba create -y -p ~/miniconda3/envs/update_genomes -f env/environment.yml
If your micromamba root prefix is already ~/miniconda3, the equivalent named-environment command is:
micromamba create -y -n update_genomes -f env/environment.yml

title: Environment (micromamba)
section: Run commands without activating the environment
text:
Run commands without activating the environment
micromamba run -p ~/miniconda3/envs/update_genomes snakemake --version
micromamba run -p ~/miniconda3/envs/update_genomes STAR --version

title: Environment (micromamba)
text:
Update the environment
If env/environment.yml changes:
micromamba update -y -p ~/miniconda3/envs/update_genomes -f env/environment.yml
./scripts/bcfngs env check

title: Environment (micromamba)
text:
Health checks
./scripts/bcfngs env check
Example output:
{
  "snakemake": "8.30.0",
  "STAR": "2.7.11b",
  "bwa": "",
  "bowtie2": "/fs/gpfs41/lv07/fileset07/home/b_cox/yeroslaviz/miniconda3/envs/update_genomes/bin/bowtie2-build-s version 2.5.5",
  "samtools": "samtools 1.23"
}

title: Environment (micromamba)
text:
Troubleshooting missing tools
If ./scripts/bcfngs env check reports a missing tool:

Update the environment:

./scripts/bcfngs env update
./scripts/bcfngs env check

If you use a non-default location, pass it explicitly:

./scripts/bcfngs --env-prefix /path/to/your/env env update
./scripts/bcfngs --env-prefix /path/to/your/env env check

Verify the package is installed in the expected environment:

micromamba list -p ~/miniconda3/envs/update_genomes | grep -Ei 'snakemake|star|bwa|bowtie2|samtools|rseqc'
micromamba run -p ~/miniconda3/envs/update_genomes snakemake --version

If needed, recreate the environment:

rm -rf ~/miniconda3/envs/update_genomes
./scripts/bcfngs env create
./scripts/bcfngs env check

title: Environment (micromamba)
text:
How-to (common scenarios)

First-time setup
./scripts/bcfngs env create
./scripts/bcfngs env check


Run a one-off command without activating
micromamba run -p ~/miniconda3/envs/update_genomes snakemake --help
micromamba run -p ~/miniconda3/envs/update_genomes STAR --version


Keep tools up to date
./scripts/bcfngs env update
./scripts/bcfngs env check
Optional lockfile:
mkdir -p env/locks
micromamba list -p ~/miniconda3/envs/update_genomes --explicit > env/locks/explicit-YYYY-MM-DD.txt


Use HPC modules for vendor tools
You can keep python and snakemake in micromamba while loading aligners from modules:
module load bclconvert/4.3.13 star bowtie2 bwa
./scripts/bcfngs --profile modules env check
./scripts/bcfngs --profile modules genomes plan
./scripts/bcfngs --profile modules genomes build --cores 20


Demultiplexing with bcl-convert
This repo does not automate demultiplexing yet. Typical usage on the cluster is:
module load bclconvert/4.3.13
bcl-convert --help

title: HOWTO
text: This is an internal HOW-TO for the workflow of the automation for the bcfngs core facility.

title: HOWTO
text:
genome indexing
This steps needs to be run only once. unless new genomes are added.
This is all run on the hpcl5002.

log-in to the server.
If not done before, activate the conda environment inside a screen, so that one can log out from the server and still run the workflow in the background. More information about the is on the github web site.
open the config.yaml file, e.g. vi /fs/pool/pool-bcfngs/scripts/config.yaml and add the genome links from the ensembl FTP site in this format:

  Dps.3.0.45:
    fasta: "ftp://ftp.ensemblgenomes.org/pub/metazoa/release-45/fasta/drosophila_pseudoobscura/dna/Drosophila_pseudoobscura.Dpse_3.0.dna.toplevel.fa.gz"
    gtf: "ftp://ftp.ensemblgenomes.org/pub/metazoa/release-45/gtf/drosophila_pseudoobscura/Drosophila_pseudoobscura.Dpse_3.0.45.gtf.gz"

run the command snakemake -nps ../scripts/getGenome_IndexGenome.Snakefile to check that the new genome is added to the workflow (the -n is a dry-run which just output the command without performing the tasks.)
run the same command without the -n - snakemake -ps ../scripts/getGenome_IndexGenome.Snakefile -j 12. This will download the fastA and gtf files from the given links and create the index files for them as well. The parameter -j sets the number pf cores used in the snakemake run. If the number of cores is smaller then the one sets for e.g. STAR, only this numner of cores will be used.

title: HOWTO
text:
Mapping
Mapping will be done with the STAR aligner for a specific genome either in a paired-end or single-end format.

Changing parameters before mapping
The following setting must be adjusted before running the script:
######## Project Setting
# To create a separate project results folder for each run, change the name of the project here.
project: "P152"
# this path would point to the raw data in fastq format. This must be changed each time for the correct data to be analyzed.
path: "/fs/pool/pool-bcfngs/fastq_files/P146/P152_ChIP_Asx_5_6/conc.fastq"
# Which organism to map the data to
org: "Dme.BDGP6.22"
The project and the path should point to the current project which is being mapped.
the org must be the organism the data is mapped against. The term must be identical to the term in the list of organisms from the config files.


running the star mapping step.
first, change the working directory to the path of the actual project on the pool-bcfngs. e.g.
cd /fs/pool/pool-bcfngs/fastq_files/P000/P000_Testrun/conc.fastq/
Next run the snakemake script first as a dry-run (-n) and if all seems well than run it for real.
e.g. 
# for single-end samples
snakemake -nps /fs/pool/pool-bcfngs/scripts/Star.MappingQuant.SingleEndFastq.Snakefile -j 50
# for paired-end samples
snakemake -ps /fs/pool/pool-bcfngs/scripts/Star.MappingQuant.PairedEndFastq.Snakefile -j 50

title: Updating genomes and indices
text:
This page is the operator guide for ./scripts/bcfngs genomes. It covers:
Environment setup is documented elsewhere. This page assumes ./scripts/bcfngs already runs on your machine or cluster.

title: Updating genomes and indices
text:
Command overview
bcfngs genomes currently exposes three subcommands:

check-updates: compare the pinned Ensembl release in configs/genomes.yaml with the latest Ensembl release visible on ftp.ensembl.org
plan: run preflight checks, snakemake --lint, and a dry-run
build: run plan, then execute the workflow for real

Inspect the wrapper help first:
./scripts/bcfngs genomes --help
./scripts/bcfngs genomes plan --help
./scripts/bcfngs genomes build --help
./scripts/bcfngs genomes check-updates --help
At the wrapper level, only build has extra arguments:
./scripts/bcfngs genomes build --help
usage: bcfngs genomes build [-h] [--cores CORES] [--organism ORGANISM]
That means:

use --cores to control total workflow parallelism
use --organism for a comma-separated subset of organism keys
use direct snakemake for advanced cases such as scheduler memory flags or building only one index type

title: Updating genomes and indices
text:
Common bcfngs genomes workflows

1) Preflight only
Use this before every real run. It checks that the YAML parses, target URLs are reachable, the output root is writable, and the Snakemake graph is valid.
./scripts/bcfngs genomes plan


2) Build all configured genomes locally
./scripts/bcfngs genomes build --cores 20
More local examples:
./scripts/bcfngs genomes build --cores 8
./scripts/bcfngs genomes build --cores 20
./scripts/bcfngs genomes build --cores 32
--cores is the total job budget for the Snakemake run. It is not the same thing as the per-tool thread count used inside STAR or bowtie2-build.


3) Build only selected organisms
Organism names must match the keys in configs/genomes.yaml exactly.
./scripts/bcfngs genomes build --cores 20 --organism Homo_sapiens
./scripts/bcfngs genomes build --cores 20 --organism Homo_sapiens,Mus_musculus
./scripts/bcfngs genomes build --cores 12 --organism Drosophila_melanogaster,Drosophila_pseudoobscura
Use this when:

you added or changed only one organism
you want to test a release bump on a smaller subset first
you do not want to rebuild every organism after a config edit



4) Run with the modules profile
If your cluster provides aligners via modules, use the modules profile:
module load bclconvert/4.3.13 star bowtie2 bwa
./scripts/bcfngs --profile modules genomes plan
./scripts/bcfngs --profile modules genomes build --cores 20
./scripts/bcfngs --profile modules genomes build --cores 20 --organism Homo_sapiens
Important: in the current implementation, --profile modules loads STAR, bwa, and bowtie2 from modules, but python and snakemake still come from the micromamba environment selected by ./scripts/bcfngs.

title: Updating genomes and indices
section: Parameters you can tune in configs/genomes.yaml
text:
Parameters you can tune in configs/genomes.yaml
The wrapper only exposes --cores and --organism, but the workflow behavior is also controlled by configs/genomes.yaml.

Global settings
genomes_root: /fs/pool/pool-bcfngs/genomes
default_threads: 16
default_ram_bytes: 168632718037

genomes_root: where sources, indices, and stable symlinks are written
default_threads: thread count used by STAR genome generation and bowtie2-build
default_ram_bytes: passed to STAR --limitGenomeGenerateRAM

Examples:

20-core workstation, one large STAR job at a time: set default_threads: 20 and run ./scripts/bcfngs genomes build --cores 20
20-core workstation, allow two medium indexing jobs in parallel: set default_threads: 10 and run ./scripts/bcfngs genomes build --cores 20
larger-memory node for STAR genome generation: increase default_ram_bytes



Release pins and index directory versions
releases:
  ensembl: 116
  ensemblgenomes:
    metazoa: 61

index_versions:
  star: "unknown"
  bwa: "unknown"
  bowtie2: "unknown"

releases.ensembl: the pinned Ensembl release used for standard Ensembl organisms
releases.ensemblgenomes.*: release pins for EnsemblGenomes divisions such as metazoa
index_versions.*: strings used in output folder names such as starIndex-<version>

If you upgrade only one tool, bump only that tool’s version string. Example: if only STAR changed, update index_versions.star and leave bwa plus bowtie2 alone.


Per-organism settings
Example:
Homo_sapiens:
  source: ensembl
  species: homo_sapiens
  assembly: GRCh38
  fasta_kind: dna.primary_assembly
  sa_index: 14
Common fields:

source: usually ensembl, sometimes ensemblgenomes
species: lowercase species path used on the FTP server
assembly: assembly label used in the remote filenames
fasta_kind: usually dna.primary_assembly or dna.toplevel
sa_index: passed to STAR --genomeSAindexNbases
fasta_url and gtf_url: explicit URLs for organisms whose source naming does not fit the default template

If you add a new organism, use its scientific name as the key, for example Arabidopsis_thaliana or Danio_rerio.

title: Updating genomes and indices
section: Check for updates and bump the genome release
text:
Check for updates and bump the genome release

Standard Ensembl release bump
First check the currently pinned release against upstream:
./scripts/bcfngs genomes check-updates
Typical output looks like:
Ensembl latest release: 117  current pinned: 116  status: UPDATE_AVAILABLE
Suggested bump: set releases.ensembl to 117 in configs/genomes.yaml
Then:

Edit configs/genomes.yaml.
Bump releases.ensembl, for example 116 to 117.
Review organism entries if any explicit fasta_url or gtf_url values need to change.
Run a dry-run first:

./scripts/bcfngs genomes plan

Rebuild:

./scripts/bcfngs genomes build --cores 20
If you want a cautious rollout, rebuild only one organism first:
./scripts/bcfngs genomes build --cores 20 --organism Homo_sapiens


EnsemblGenomes or explicit URL updates
./scripts/bcfngs genomes check-updates checks releases.ensembl. It does not audit every explicit fasta_url or gtf_url entry for you.
For organisms defined with:

source: ensemblgenomes
explicit fasta_url
explicit gtf_url

review those URLs manually, update the release pin under releases.ensemblgenomes, and then run plan plus build again.

title: Updating genomes and indices
section: Rebuild indices after a tool upgrade
text:
Rebuild indices after a tool upgrade
When the genome sequence did not change but an indexer changed, update only the version labels for the affected tools:
index_versions:
  star: "2.7.11b"
  bwa: "0.7.18"
  bowtie2: "2.5.5"
Then rebuild:
./scripts/bcfngs genomes plan
./scripts/bcfngs genomes build --cores 20
Successful builds update the stable symlinks automatically:

/fs/pool/pool-bcfngs/genomes/<Organism>/starIndex
/fs/pool/pool-bcfngs/genomes/<Organism>/bwaIndex
/fs/pool/pool-bcfngs/genomes/<Organism>/bowtie2Index

These symlinks point to versioned directories such as starIndex-2.7.11b.

title: Updating genomes and indices
text:
Advanced: direct snakemake usage
Use direct snakemake when you need options that ./scripts/bcfngs genomes does not currently expose.

Build only one index type
The wrapper always targets the full workflow. To build only one tool, target the desired output file directly.
Replace the version placeholder with the matching value from index_versions in configs/genomes.yaml.
Only STAR for Homo_sapiens:
snakemake -pr -j 20 \
  -s workflow/genomes.smk \
  --configfile configs/genomes.yaml \
  /fs/pool/pool-bcfngs/genomes/Homo_sapiens/starIndex-<STAR_VERSION>/SA
Only bwa for Homo_sapiens:
snakemake -pr -j 20 \
  -s workflow/genomes.smk \
  --configfile configs/genomes.yaml \
  /fs/pool/pool-bcfngs/genomes/Homo_sapiens/bwaIndex-<BWA_VERSION>/Homo_sapiens.bwt
Only bowtie2 for Homo_sapiens:
snakemake -pr -j 20 \
  -s workflow/genomes.smk \
  --configfile configs/genomes.yaml \
  /fs/pool/pool-bcfngs/genomes/Homo_sapiens/bowtie2Index-<BOWTIE2_VERSION>/Homo_sapiens.1.bt2
These commands still pull in prerequisites such as source downloads, but they avoid rebuilding every index type.


Change memory-related behavior
There are two different layers here:

default_ram_bytes in configs/genomes.yaml controls STAR --limitGenomeGenerateRAM
scheduler submission flags such as SLURM --mem or --mem-per-cpu are outside the bcfngs genomes wrapper

For a permanent STAR memory change, edit:
default_ram_bytes: 250000000000
If you need scheduler-specific memory requests, run snakemake directly using your site profile or executor settings. For example, on SLURM-oriented setups this is often expressed via default resources rather than via the bcfngs wrapper:
snakemake -pr \
  --executor slurm \
  --jobs 20 \
  --default-resources mem_mb=180000 \
  -s workflow/genomes.smk \
  --configfile configs/genomes.yaml
Or, if your cluster policy is based on memory per CPU:
snakemake -pr \
  --executor slurm \
  --jobs 20 \
  --default-resources mem_mb_per_cpu=8000 \
  -s workflow/genomes.smk \
  --configfile configs/genomes.yaml
Adjust these examples to your local cluster conventions. The current ./scripts/bcfngs genomes command does not parse --mem or --mem-per-cpu directly.

title: Branch and Publish Workflow (Local Notes)
text:
codex/hpc-test: code and config only.
gh-pages: docs source + rendered site output only.

title: Branch and Publish Workflow (Local Notes)
text:
codex/hpc-test: code and config only.
gh-pages: docs source + rendered site output only.

title: Branch and Publish Workflow (Local Notes)
text:
Allowed files by branch

codex/hpc-test examples:
scripts/
workflow/
configs/
env/
Snakefiles and helper code
gh-pages examples:
*.qmd, *.Rmd, _quarto.yml
docs/ rendered output

title: Branch and Publish Workflow (Local Notes)
text:
Daily code workflow (strict split)
git switch codex/hpc-test
# edit code/config only
git add <code_or_config_files>
git commit -m "code: ..."
git push origin codex/hpc-test

title: Branch and Publish Workflow (Local Notes)
text:
Daily docs publish workflow (strict split)
git switch gh-pages
git pull origin gh-pages
# edit docs source only
quarto render
git add *.qmd *.Rmd _quarto.yml docs/
git commit -m "docs: publish site update"
git push origin gh-pages

title: Branch and Publish Workflow (Local Notes)
section: If docs were edited by mistake on codex/hpc-test
text:
If docs were edited by mistake on codex/hpc-test
Move docs work to gh-pages without committing docs on codex/hpc-test.
# 1) While on codex/hpc-test, stash docs-related files only
git stash push -u -m "move-docs-to-gh-pages" -- \
  index.Rmd conda.Rmd HOWTO.Rmd environment.qmd snakemake.Rmd updating_genomes.qmd \
  _quarto.yml docs/

# 2) Switch to docs branch and apply
git switch gh-pages
git pull origin gh-pages
git stash pop

# 3) Publish
quarto render
git add *.qmd *.Rmd _quarto.yml docs/
git commit -m "docs: publish site update"
git push origin gh-pages

# 4) Return to code branch
git switch codex/hpc-test

title: Branch and Publish Workflow (Local Notes)
text:
Conflict note (docs/search.json)
If stash apply/pop conflicts on docs/search.json while on gh-pages:
git checkout --theirs docs/search.json
git add docs/search.json
Then continue staging/commit normally.

title: Branch and Publish Workflow (Local Notes)
text:
Non-negotiable rule

Never commit docs/ output to codex/hpc-test.
Never commit code/config pipeline changes to gh-pages.

title: index
text:
The repository contains a conda-snakemake workflow for the mapping of the concatenated fastq file created at the BCF of the MPIB.
This README file for the sequencing center to help them run a preliminary analysis on the sequenced samples
The file will contains instruction on how to create a conda environment with the needed tools for the mapping and will also include snakemake for the automation of the scripts.

title: index
text:
steps to do
Preparing the genomes for mapping

Download the genomes and the annotations from the FTP server (Ensembl genome builds are used here.)
For each genome, an index for the star aligner is created.

This two steps should be done only once, when the workflow was set up. It only needs to be re-run, when a new organism is being added, or when newer genome build of exiting organisms or a newer version of the STAR mapping tool are needed.
Mapping step

Map the samples with the needed parameter.
Index the fastA file to create a chromSize file for the bigwig conversion.
Convert the bam files to wig and then to bigwig.

points to consider: If this is only a preliminary analysis -

Would it make more sense to take only 50% of the fastq file for a faster analysis / visualization
Do they need the bam files also - deleting them would save a lot of storage space

The examples in this document are based on fictive data as well as a fictional project named Project0815. When a new project is done, this term must be changed with the proper name of the project, wherever it is mentioned.

title: index
text:
folder structure
The workflow works from within the folder of the concatenated fastq files created after running the bcl2fastq script from the project management website (in the core facility here, this folder is called conc.fastq).

A config file is saved under /fs/pool/pool-bcfngs/scripts/config.yaml, where all the mapping and genome parameters are saved.
The genome indexing step will run only once and creates a folder named genomes/ on the /fs/pool/pool-bcfngs/. Within this folder all the fastA and the gtf files are saved as well as a folder for each organism, containing the star-indexed files.
The mapping step expect to find within the working directory the files with the pattern .conc.R1.fastq.gz, as this is the current pattern given by the sequencing facility. (If, in the future, this will be changed, the search pattern must be changed as well).

There are two separate files for single-ended and paired-ended data sets. The single-ended one expects to find only .conc.R1.fastq.gz, while the paired-end data set must also contain .conc.R2.fastq.gz files.

title: index
text:
Workflow
The workflow uses a micromamba environment with the snakemake tool (based on python). The advantage of micromamba is that it creates an isolated environment, separated from the rest of the tools installed on the system. This gives better control over the tool versions used in the workflow, with fewer conflicts from older system installations. To learn more about micromamba, take a look at the mamba documentation.
snakemake offers a lot of advantages as well. It allows better controls over different steps of the analysis, easier automation of the workflow and reduce running time through parallelization of the steps, just to mention some of them. to learn more about snakemake take a look at the snakemake doc site.

environment
snakemake


wigToBigwig
First the bam files are converted into wig files. This is done using the bam2wig.py script from the rseqc-software package.
In order for the automatic conversion of wig to bigwig files to work, one must have the tool wigToBigwig from the UCSC repository installed and in the path. As we are working with micromamba, the easiest place to put it is the path of the environment. In this case the path would be under ~/miniconda3/envs/ENVNAME/bin/. So far I have copied the file manually to the folder, but I have now added it to the environment.yaml file, which is ran during the environment creation step. This was not tested yet!.
TO DO
In this case it would probably be better if the indexed genomes would already be saved on the server for various organisms, as it doesn’t make sense to download one each time. We sort of know which organisms are needed in most cases.

human
mouse
zebra fish
fruit fly
yeast
worm (C. elegans)

as well as which mappers we need

star
bwa
bowtie2

title: conda
text:
The first step is to create a conda environment, containing the necessary tools for the required analysis. In this workflow, we use the snakemake package to help running the workflow, as well as some mappers. To learn more about the conda environment and its advantages, take a look at the conda web site.
Environments can be created with the help of a file, e.g. environment.yaml using the following command
conda env create --name Project0815 --file environment.yaml
This file would look like that:
name: Project0815
channels:
   - bioconda
   - conda-forge
   - r
   - bioinfo
dependencies:
   - snakemake-minimal =5.2.4
   - python =3.6
   - star =2.7.2c
   - samtools =1.9
   - bowtie2 =2.3.5
   - bwa =0.7.17
The first row of the file contains the name of the project, i.e. the name of the conda environment created. Next are the channels listed, from which the conda environment should look for the tools needed for the analysis.
the last part of the environment file is a list of the needed dependencies. Each one of them has its version attached to it. This can be very helpful, when a data set needs to be re-analyzed to check for problems or conflicts with older/newer results or for debugging purposes. In this workflow we will use the snakemake package as well as python version 3.6. we also install here the mappers star, bwa and bowtie2. WE also need the samtool package for the manipulation of the bam files (sorting, filtering, etc.).
After this was done one must activate the environment using the command
source activate Project0815
If one look at the command line one can now see this
(Project0815) USER@SERVERNAME:Project0815$ ..
The brackets on the left side must contains the name of the environment.
If, after the environment was created, new tools must be added, they can just be added to the list of tools in the environment.yaml file and than the conda environment must be updated, using the command
conda env update -f environment.yaml 
The conda environment should be created only once, but with each login one must activate it, so that the snakemake script can be run from the correct paths.

title: conda
text:
The first step is to create a conda environment, containing the necessary tools for the required analysis. In this workflow, we use the snakemake package to help running the workflow, as well as some mappers. To learn more about the conda environment and its advantages, take a look at the conda web site.
Environments can be created with the help of a file, e.g. environment.yaml using the following command
conda env create --name Project0815 --file environment.yaml
This file would look like that:
name: Project0815
channels:
   - bioconda
   - conda-forge
   - r
   - bioinfo
dependencies:
   - snakemake-minimal =5.2.4
   - python =3.6
   - star =2.7.2c
   - samtools =1.9
   - bowtie2 =2.3.5
   - bwa =0.7.17
The first row of the file contains the name of the project, i.e. the name of the conda environment created. Next are the channels listed, from which the conda environment should look for the tools needed for the analysis.
the last part of the environment file is a list of the needed dependencies. Each one of them has its version attached to it. This can be very helpful, when a data set needs to be re-analyzed to check for problems or conflicts with older/newer results or for debugging purposes. In this workflow we will use the snakemake package as well as python version 3.6. we also install here the mappers star, bwa and bowtie2. WE also need the samtool package for the manipulation of the bam files (sorting, filtering, etc.).
After this was done one must activate the environment using the command
source activate Project0815
If one look at the command line one can now see this
(Project0815) USER@SERVERNAME:Project0815$ ..
The brackets on the left side must contains the name of the environment.
If, after the environment was created, new tools must be added, they can just be added to the list of tools in the environment.yaml file and than the conda environment must be updated, using the command
conda env update -f environment.yaml 
The conda environment should be created only once, but with each login one must activate it, so that the snakemake script can be run from the correct paths.

title: snakemake
text:
This part is still work in progress.
This repository uses snakemake for:
For non-experts, prefer the single wrapper command ./scripts/bcfngs (runs checks, then snakemake).
By default, ./scripts/bcfngs uses the micromamba environment at ~/miniconda3/envs/update_genomes. If your environment is elsewhere, add --env-prefix /path/to/env.

title: snakemake
text:
Quickstart
# 1) create/update env (only once, then as needed)
./scripts/bcfngs env create
./scripts/bcfngs env check

# 2) (optional) check if a newer Ensembl release exists
./scripts/bcfngs genomes check-updates

# 3) preflight checks + snakemake lint + dry-run
./scripts/bcfngs genomes plan

# 4) build genomes + indices (real run)
./scripts/bcfngs genomes build --cores 20

title: snakemake
text:
Configuration files
The workflow configuration is split into two files:

Genome and indexing configuration: configs/genomes.yaml

Organism identifiers are scientific names (e.g. Homo_sapiens, Mus_musculus).
Genome sources are pinned to a specific Ensembl release (default: 116).
Index output directories are versioned via index_versions.

Per-project run configuration (mapping): configs/run.yaml

Project ID, input path, organism, threads, etc.



Notes

Genome indexing uses workflow/genomes.smk (not the legacy getGenome_IndexGenome.Snakefile).
Mapping uses the STAR mapping Snakefiles and expects /fs/pool/pool-bcfngs/genomes/<Organism>/starIndex to exist (symlink to the versioned index).

title: snakemake
text:
How-to (cookbook)

Build all genomes + indices
./scripts/bcfngs genomes plan
./scripts/bcfngs genomes build --cores 20


Build only a subset (faster)
./scripts/bcfngs genomes build --cores 20 --organism Homo_sapiens,Mus_musculus


Ensembl has a newer release (bump pinned release)

Check:

./scripts/bcfngs genomes check-updates

If an update is available, edit configs/genomes.yaml and bump:


releases.ensembl: 116 → 117 (etc.)


Then:

./scripts/bcfngs genomes plan
./scripts/bcfngs genomes build --cores 20


New STAR/bwa/bowtie2 version (rebuild indices)

Update tools:

./scripts/bcfngs env update
./scripts/bcfngs env check

Update configs/genomes.yaml:


set index_versions.star, index_versions.bwa, index_versions.bowtie2 (these strings are used in output folder names)


Rebuild:

./scripts/bcfngs genomes build --cores 20


Use HPC modules for aligners (fallback)
This still uses the micromamba env for python/snakemake, but loads vendor tools via modules:
module load bclconvert/4.3.13 star bowtie2 bwa
./scripts/bcfngs --profile modules env check
./scripts/bcfngs --profile modules genomes plan
./scripts/bcfngs --profile modules genomes build --cores 20

title: snakemake
text:
Genome download + indexing
Workflow file: workflow/genomes.smk

Mapping the samples with the STAR
mapping the samples in a paired-end format
This script contains three (four) steps. in the first step the fastq samples are mapped to the indexed genome. Then, they are being indexed. Last, after creating a chromosome-size file, the bam files are converted into bigwig files for better visualization using the UCSC browser.
We have two separate scripts for single-end and paired-end runs. The PE run looks for both R1 and R2 fastq files, while the SE script will ignore all R2 samples.
After the environment was created and activated, one must change to the directory of the concatenated fast files. E.g.
cd /fs/pool/pool-bcfngs/fastq_files/P000/P000_Testrun/conc.fastq/
To run mapping, update configs/run.yaml in the repo, then run one of these Snakefiles.
# for single-end samples
snakemake -nps /path/to/repo/Star.MappingQuant.SingleEndFastq.Snakefile -j 50
# for paired-end samples
snakemake -nps /path/to/repo/Star.MappingQuant.PairedEndFastq.Snakefile -j 50

-n - dry-run. This parameter will only output the command, but execute them. This should be used to test the parameters, but must be removed for the analysis to be done.
-p - Print the commands to be execute.
-s - the name of the snakeake file to be executed.
-j - #cores. How many cores should be used in the run.
